name: The Monastery Meeting
description: Commander Serel wants to meet General Cross at the monastery — neutral ground, accessible from both approaches. Abbot Solan needs to know a meeting between the commanders of the two factions is coming.
accept_text: She has written nothing down. The arrangements are in her head, which is where she keeps things she does not want filed. 'The monastery has been a neutral site for two hundred years,' she says. 'There are protocols for it — both sides recognize its neutrality, neither sends armed units within a kilometer, neither requests access to its records. The Abbot maintains this by treating both sides identically, which means neither side has leverage.' She pauses. 'Solan has hosted informal communications before — not at command level, but individual soldiers, researchers, people who needed to cross between the factions for personal reasons.' She looks at the map. 'I need you to go to the monastery and tell Abbot Solan that a formal command-level meeting has been requested, that both parties have agreed, and that we are requesting the monastery as neutral venue.' She pauses. 'Give him this.' She hands you a sealed envelope with no external markings. 'It is from me formally, addressed to him formally, requesting the meeting on behalf of Terran command and acknowledging that the request is conditional on his consent. The monastery is sovereign in this.' She meets your eyes. 'He will say yes. He's been hoping for this kind of conversation for thirty years. But the form matters — we ask, he consents, the meeting happens on his terms in his house.' A pause. 'Tell him the date we've proposed. Tell him I will travel without escort. Tell him Cross has agreed to the same.' She is quiet. 'Tell him we are trying to do something that we are not certain we know how to do, and that we would be grateful for his guidance on the form it should take.'
complete_text: Solan reads the letter standing at the entrance to the great hall where he reads formal communications, which is all of them — he has maintained for thirty years that formal communications deserve formal acknowledgment. When he finishes, he is quiet for longer than he usually is. 'Serel,' he says. Not Commander — the name. The way he uses names when he's thinking about a person rather than a title. 'Cross.' Same. He folds the letter carefully. 'I have been abbot of this monastery for thirty-one years.' He looks at the hall. 'In that time, I have made forty-seven accommodations for individuals crossing the armistice line — scholars, medics, families, soldiers who needed to end something that the war left unfinished. None of them was command level.' He looks at you. 'This is different. If Cross and Serel sit in this hall — this is what the armistice was built toward. Not a ceasefire. A conversation between the people responsible for the ceasefire about what comes after it.' He is very still. 'Yes. Tell them: the monastery consents. Tell them the date works.' He goes to the desk and writes something on heavy paper. 'Give this to both of them separately. It is my formal acknowledgment of the request and the terms under which the monastery will host the meeting.' He hands you two sealed envelopes. 'The terms are: both parties arrive without weapons, without escort, without aide. The meeting is private — I will be present as host, no other monastery personnel. Nothing said in this hall is recorded.' He pauses. 'Tell them: the monastery has been waiting for this conversation. We have been holding the space for it. We are ready.'
